clusters.title: Mainland Australia’s first suspected case of deadly H5N1 investigated after seabird tests positive for bird flu
clusters.neutral_headline: Suspected H5 bird flu detected in Australia
bullet_summary: The brown skua, a subantartic species, was found at Cape Le Grand national park on Sunday and has since died, the WA government said on Friday night
Initial testing at a WA laboratory returned a “suspected positive result for avian influenza”, the agriculture minister, Julie Collins, said
A wild migratory bird in Western Australia has returned a suspected positive result for avian influenza, which has devastated populations of seabirds, seals and other animals across the world

items.title: Mainland Australia’s first suspected case of deadly H5N1 investigated after seabird tests positive for bird flu
items.standfirst: Agriculture minister says no evidence of mass mortality or infection in poultry as WA authorities say a second bird is sick and being tested A migratory seabird found in southern Western Australia has tested positive for avian influenza with further testing under way to confirm if it is Australia’s first mainland case of the deadly H5N1 strain. The brown skua, a subantartic species, was found at Cape Le Grand national park on Sunday and has since died, the WA government said on Friday night. A second bird, a giant petrel, is sick and being tested.Initial testing at a WA laboratory returned a “suspected positive result for avian influenza”, the agriculture minister, Julie Collins, said. Continue reading...
items.content: Agriculture minister says no evidence of mass mortality or infection in poultry as WA authorities say a second bird is sick and being tested A migratory seabird found in southern Western Australia has tested positive for avian influenza with further testing under way to confirm if it is Australia’s first mainland case of the deadly H5N1 strain. The brown skua, a subantartic species, was found at Cape Le Grand national park on Sunday and has since died, the WA government said on Friday night. A second bird, a giant petrel, is sick and being tested.Initial testing at a WA laboratory returned a “suspected positive result for avian influenza”, the agriculture minister, Julie Collins, said. Continue reading...

items.content: A wild migratory bird in Western Australia has returned a suspected positive result for avian influenza, which has devastated populations of seabirds, seals and other animals across the world.
